coredata.dc:title: Inhibitory effects of carbon quantum dots towards hen egg white lysozyme amyloidogenesis through formation of a stable protein complex
coredata.dc:creator: Prabhu, M.P. Taraka
Sarkar, Nandini
coredata.dc:description: Proteins, under certain circumstances such as defective quality control mechanism, mutations and altered environmental conditions, undergo misfolding and assemble into highly ordered beta-sheet structured fibrillar aggregates called amyloid fibrils. Formation of amyloid is seen in most of the protein linked degenerative diseases like Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, Type II diabetes mellitus and many more. Amyloid fibril forms via intermediate state(s), and is known to follow a nucleated condensation polymerization mechanism. Though extensive research is being carried out towards finding a therapeutic solution to the amyloidosis, an effective treatment to these diseases still remains elusive and also the mechanism of amyloidogenesis largely remains unclear. In recent times, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are gaining the attention of researchers due to their semi-conductive nature, excellent physio-chemical properties, high surface to volume ratio, optical properties and mainly bio-compatibility. In the current study, we have synthesized CQDs from commonly available kitchen spice mix and explored their role in amyloidogenesis using hen egg white lysozyme (HEWL) as a model protein. The results clearly demonstrate the amyloid inhibitory as well as disaggregation potential of CQD by forming a stable complex with HEWL and thereby increasing the energy barrier for the aggregation process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Protein quality control
Amyloidosis
Neurogenerative diseases
Carbon quantum dots
Hen egg white lysozyme

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: HEWL amyloid inhibition and disaggregation study
HEWL amyloid fibril morphology and conformation
HEWL and CQD interaction study using fluorescence quenching
Equilibrium unfolding of HEWL protein in presence of CQD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization studies of the CQDs- (a) UV–Visible Absorption spectra, (b) Fluorescence emission spectra, (c) TEM Image, (d) FTIR spectra, (e) XRD pattern, and (f) SAED pattern.

(a) Elution profile of the as-synthesized CQDs from Sephadex G 25 column (b) ThT assay of the eluted fractions. Experimental conditions were pH 2.7 Gly-HCl buffer, [HEWL] = 5 μM, CQD = 300 μl, ThT = 20 μM, Temperature = 65 °C, RPM = 850.

ThT fluorescence assay of HEWL amyloidogenesis with the CQDs showing (a) amyloid inhibition, (b) amyloid disaggregation. Experimental conditions were pH 2.7 Gly-HCl buffer, [HEWL] = 5 μM, [CQD] = 300 μl, ThT = 20 μM, Temperature = 65 °C, RPM = 850.

HEWL amyloid inhibition study using ThT fluorescence with CQD added at (a) 0 h, (b) 1 h, (c) 2 h, (d) 3 h. Experimental conditions were pH 2.7 Gly-HCl buffer, [HEWL] = 5 μM, ThT = 20 μM, [CQD] = 350 μl, Temperature = 65 °C, RPM = 850. The amyloid inhibition percentage was 50%, 30%, 14% and 10% when CQDs added at 0 h, 1 h, 2 h and 3 h respectively.

FESEM Images of (a) pre-fibril, (b) pre-fibril with CQD, (c) Mature fibril, (d) Mature fibril with CQDs. Experimental conditions were pH 2.7 Gly-HCl buffer, [HEWL] = 5 μM, Temperature = 65 °C, RPM = 850.
(a) Far UV CD spectra and (b) FTIR spectra of HEWL in presence and absence of CQDs. Experimental conditions were pH 2.7 Gly-HCl buffer, [HEWL] = 5 μM, Temperature = 65 °C, RPM = 850.

(a) FRET phenomena of the CQDs and HEWL when excited at tryptophan excitation wavelength (295 nm) and emission range was set in range of (300–650 nm) (b) Stern Volmer quenching plot of HEWL in presence of CQD as the quencher, done at different temperatures. Experimental conditions were pH 2.7 Gly-HCl buffer, [HEWL] = 5 μM, Temperature = 65 °C, RPM = 850.

(a) Guanidine hydrochloride (GuHCl) mediated equilibrium unfolding studies of HEWL in presence and absence of the CQDs at pH -2 (b) Energy diagram showing the probable thermodynamic mechanism of amyloid inhibition of the CQDs by for a stable complex with HEWL. Experimental conditions were pH 2.7 Gly-HCl buffer, [HEWL] = 5 μM, Temperature = 65 °C, RPM = 850.
Schematic representation of the mode of action of the CQDs on HEWL amyloidogenesis.
Predicted secondary structural content of HEWL in presence and absence of CQDs obtained from Far UV CD spectra using DichroWeb online server.
Calculated quenching rate and binding parameters obtained from the Stern-Volmer plot.

head.ce:title: Inhibitory effects of carbon quantum dots towards hen egg white lysozyme amyloidogenesis through formation of a stable protein complex

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology and Medical Engineering, National Institute of Technology Rourkela, Rourkela 769008, Odisha, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biotechnology and Medical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology and Medical Engineering, National Institute of Technology Rourkela, Rourkela- 769008, Odisha, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Proteins, under certain circumstances such as defective quality control mechanism, mutations and altered environmental conditions, undergo misfolding and assemble into highly ordered beta-sheet structured fibrillar aggregates called amyloid fibrils. Formation of amyloid is seen in most of the protein linked degenerative diseases like Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, Type II diabetes mellitus and many more. Amyloid fibril forms via intermediate state(s), and is known to follow a nucleated condensation polymerization mechanism. Though extensive research is being carried out towards finding a therapeutic solution to the amyloidosis, an effective treatment to these diseases still remains elusive and also the mechanism of amyloidogenesis largely remains unclear. In recent times, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are gaining the attention of researchers due to their semi-conductive nature, excellent physio-chemical properties, high surface to volume ratio, optical properties and mainly bio-compatibility. In the current study, we have synthesized CQDs from commonly available kitchen spice mix and explored their role in amyloidogenesis using hen egg white lysozyme (HEWL) as a model protein. The results clearly demonstrate the amyloid inhibitory as well as disaggregation potential of CQD by forming a stable complex with HEWL and thereby increasing the energy barrier for the aggregation process.

ce:section.ce:para: For a cell to function normally, the most fundamental and crucial process is the ability of proteins to undergo correct folding into their native states. As this natural phenomenon is quite complex, there exists a great chance for the protein to misfold. To avoid such misfolding, the cells are evolved with several intracellular regulatory systems to sense and act against those misfolded proteins, such as the chaperone system, which is found in majority of the cell types and sub-cellular compartments. However, despite this regulatory system, the protein misfolding and aggregation processes happen in the intra and extra cellular environments, triggered by conditions like ageing, mutation, changes in the local environmental and so on. Sometimes, these aggregates demonstrate an ordered, β sheet rich, stable, fibrillar arrangements, which are known as “amyloids” [1,2]. Formation of amyloids leads to the onset of various degenerative diseases like Alzheimer's Disease (AD), Parkinson's Disease (PD), Type-II diabetes, Cystic fibrosis, Creutz Feldt-Jakob's and Huntington Disease. Keeping in view the adverse effects caused by the amyloidosis, researchers have been studying the various ways and means for the inhibition of the amyloid formation. Till now many compounds such as anti-oxidants, small molecules, and vitamins have been identified with inhibitory activity towards the amyloid formation in-vitro [3]. With the intense research towards amyloidosis now we can be able to study the structure, kinetics of transient intermediate species and design the inhibitors [4,5]. However, even with the extensive data available so far, an effective treatment against amyloidosis remains elusive. In recent times, nanomedicines owing to their properties like high surface area to volume ratio, ease of synthesis and functionalization, bio-compatibility and biostability, they have emerged as potential tool for various applications like bioimaging, drug delivery, biosensors and so on. Among nanomedicines, carbon quantum dots (CQD) are of particular interest due to their nontoxicity and unique optical properties. So far, many natural and chemical compound have been the source for the synthesis of CQD such as Glucose, Cellulose, raw cashew gum, bagasse, goose feathers, egg yolk oil, alcohols, soymilk, naked oats, carbonized chitosan and citric acid [6–16]. Different plant parts like Neem gum, bamboo, coriander, garlic, tulsi, turmeric smoke, coconut husk, food caramels [17–24] and many a more. Recent reports have demonstrated inhibitory role of Graphene quantum dots (GQD) against Amyloid-β peptide amyloidogenesis [25]. In a recent review the authors discussed the structures, pathology associated with the human islet amyloid peptide (hIAPP) and also pointed out the opportunities for the inhibition of the aggregation [26]. In another study, disaggregation ability of the curcumin derivative towards the hIAPP was highlighted [27]. Another study reported the inhibitory effects of fluorine conjugated GQDs towards amyloidogenesis of human islet amyloid polypeptide (hIAPP) [28]. The same hIAPP fibrillation was inhibited by the action of CQD synthesized with a combination of Tyrosine and Citric acid [29]. The CQD by carbonization of sucrose with oil acid showed strong inhibitory action towards human insulin fibrillation [30]. In another study human insulin fibril were targeted with CQD synthesized from carbon powder [31]. Sodium fusidate inhibitory role against the silk fibroin offers a new perspective for the human lens material disaggregation [32]. CQD synthesized through one step hydrothermal treatment with sodium citrate dihydrate and ammonium bicarbonate showed disaggregation activity towards fully formed HEWL fibrils [33]. In an interesting work, it was discussed about the role of HEWL amyloid seeds towards the aggregation of Bovine serum albumin amyloid formation at physiological temperature [34]. The permeability of CQDs across the lipid bilayer was also made easy with some modifications with organic molecules, where the in-vivo live cell imaging was made possible as well. Further, the biocompatible nature of the CQDs made them an attractive therapeutic target [34,35]. Moreover, it was also mentioned that CQDs are insensitive towards heavy metals unlike quantum dots from CdSe and CdS [35,36]. But, the underlying mechanism of action towards amyloids remains unclear and demands further investigation. In the present study, we have used Hen egg white lysozyme (HEWL) as model protein which is homologous to human lysozyme known to be responsible for hereditary systemic amyloidosis. HEWL is a small protein (14.3 kDa) with four di-sulfide bonds, whose amyloid forming conditions are well characterized. The CQD were synthesized from kitchen spice mixture consisting of Syzygium aromaticum, Cinnamomum verum, Elettaria cardamomum and Larus nobilis. These spices possess anti-oxidant activity [37,39,40], antipyretic activity [38], Anti-mutagenic activity [39]. The CQD were explored for their anti-amyloidogenic potential towards HEWL amyloids, using various biophysical approaches. The mechanistic details of their effects were also elucidated in order to get more insights towards the process of amyloidogenesis.

Hen egg white lysozyme (HEWL) purity ≥90%, guanidine hydrochloride purity ≥99%, Thioflavin T were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Glycine purity≥99%, Hydrogen chloride from Merck purity≥35%, Di‑sodium hydrogen phosphate purity ≥98.5%, Sodium di‑hydrogen phosphate purity ≥98%, Sodium chloride purity ≥99.5% purchased from HiMedia Laboratory Chemicals (India), hydrothermal autoclave Teflon coated (50 ml) purchased from ANTS ceramics (India). Spices for the study were purchased from local grocery store.

The species S. aromaticum, C. verum, E. cardamomum and Larus nobilis were used as source for preparation of the CQDs. Equal weight proportions of the spices were made into fine powder and stored. 0.8 g of spice mix powder added to 30 ml millipore water. The mixture is subjected to sonication for 10 min with 50 Hz frequency at 1 s on and off. The undissolved part of solution is separated by centrifugation. The left-over solution is transferred into 50 ml Teflon coated hydrothermal autoclave and exposed to 190 °C temperature for 4 h. After the stipulated time duration, the autoclave allowed to reach room temperature naturally. Filtration and followed by Sephadex (G-25) column separation of the solution are performed. Fractions collected were stored at 4 °C for further study.

The UV–Visible Spectroscopy recorded by UV–Visible Spectrophotometer (Varian 100, Agilent Technology), Fluorescence behavior of CQD was recorded by using Fluorescence Spectrometer (Jobin Yvon fluoromax-4P). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed by FTIR Spectroscope (Bruker, Alpha E), X-ray diffraction pattern of the CQDs was recorded by Multipurpose X-Ray diffraction system (Rigaku, Japan & Ultima -IV), Transmission electronic microscopy image was recorded from TEM (FEI, Tecnai G2 TF30-ST), size of the CQDs was recorded by using nanoparticle size and zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, NanoZS90).

For the preparation of HEWL amyloid we followed the method of Gazova et. al, (2018) with some modifications. Briefly, 35 μM HEWL was dissolved in freshly prepared 70 mM Glycine-HCl buffer with pH 2.7. The solution was subjected to amyloid forming conditions of 65 °C, 840 rpm agitation for 5 h. on hot plate magnetic stirrer (Tarsons). To investigate the effect of CQD on HEWL fibrillation, the sample containing final protein concentration of 5 μM and CQD was prepared and incubated as mentioned above. Here by the final concentration of the protein in all the experiments remain 5 μM and all the experiments carried out as triplicate unless mentioned.

ThT, widely used amyloid specific dye, shows increase in its fluorescence intensity around 480–490 nm when excited at 440 nm due to its binding with fibrillar structures [41]. As mentioned in the above method the aliquots collected at regular intervals. These aliquots were incubated in the absence and presence of CQD and mixed with dye, keeping the protein concentration and ThT concentrations 5 and 20 μM respectively. For amyloid inhibition assay the CQD were added at the starting of the experiment and for the amyloid disaggregation study the CQD were added after formation of amyloid. When increasing volumes of CQDs were added equivalent volume of buffer was removed in order to keep HEWL concentration constant. Samples were incubated in dark for 30 min before being scanned. The emission range was set around 450–700 nm and silt width 5 nm was maintained. The readings were recorded using Horiba Duetta Absorbance and Fluorescence Spectrophotometer.

FTIR spectra used mainly for knowing the functional groups present at the surface of a molecule or substance and also to know the modifications in the structural arrangement for biomolecules. The fibrils were prepared in the presence and absence of the CQD. Aliquots were collected and subjected for the spectral scan in the range of 500–4000 cm−1.

To investigate the change in the secondary structures of the protein upon interaction with a ligand Circular Dichroism spectrum will be used. In our study, HEWL protein subjected to amyloid fibrillation process in the presence and absence of CQD, the change in the secondary structure content was recorded by JASCO J-1500 model Circular Dichroism Spectrophotometer in the far-UV range of 200–260 nm having 5 mm path length cell. The data was analyzed using DichroWeb online server.

FEI Nova NanoSEM 450 model Field Emission Scanning Electronic Microscope (FESEM) was used to capture the images of HEWL proto-fibrils and fibrils formation in presence and absence of the CQD. The sample was prepared as a thin film on a glass slide by air drying method in an incubator. The scale of images was kept same for all the samples.

HEWL protein was incubated at desired concentrations of guanidine hydrochloride (Gu-HCl) in 70 mM Glycine-HCL Buffer of pH 2.7 for approximately 24 h at 25 °C in the presence and absence of CQDs. CQDs were added to the samples to understand their role in the protein unfolding. The final concentration of the protein 5 μM was maintained same in all the samples and the concentration of Gu-HCl is varied from 0 to 6 M. The incubated samples were evaluated through intrinsic fluorescence, excitation wavelength was fixed at 295 nm and emission wavelength range was set from 310 to 400 nm, silt width of 5 nm was maintained. Finally, the graph of protein fraction unfolded against Gu-HCl concentration in the absence and presence of CQD was plotted [42].

Binding of CQD to proteins has been investigated by Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and intrinsic fluorescence of proteins. The intrinsic fluorescence indicates the fluorescence of Tryptophan residue fluorescence present in proteins. Therefore, quenching effect over tryptophan fluorescence of HEWL by adding CQD reveals an information related to the interactive nature of CQD with HEWL. For observing FRET effect protein sample along with CQD excited at 295 nm wavelength and the emission range of 300–650 nm wavelength. The duration of excitation was 300 s with a time gap of 50 s. The process of quenching always described by the Stern-Volmer equation shown as Eq. (1) and the binding parameters such as association constant (KA) and number of binding sites (n) can be calculated with equation shown as Eq. (2).
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
∗
Q
= 1 +
K q
∗
τ 0
∗
Q
(2)
ln
F 0
▬ F
/ F
= ln
K A
+ n ∗ ln
Q

In the above equations F0 and F indicates intrinsic fluorescence values of HEWL in absence and presence of quencher respectively. Kq is bimolecular quenching rate constant. τ0 is average lifetime of biomolecule with quencher (τ0 = 10−8 s). KSV is Stern-Volmer constant. [Q] is quencher (CQD) concentration and n indicate number of binding sites [43].

The CQD were successfully synthesized from natural and cheaper sources e.g., kitchen spices. The as prepared CQD were readily soluble in aqueous solution and were found to be stable for several weeks. Thus, the method presented here can be adopted as an easy method for synthesis of CQD. The as prepared CQD exhibited a Quantum Yield of 11%. The quantum yield was calculated using Quinine Sulfate as standard reference with the Eq. (3) [18,44].
(3)
QY =
QY r
∗
I /
I r
∗
A r
/ A
∗
ƞ r
/ ƞ

Where r refers to the standard reference. I, A and ƞ represents the fluorescence emission intensity, absorbance intensity and refractive index of the solvent respectively. Quinine Sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4. The Fig. 1(a) represents UV–Visible absorption spectrum of the as prepared CQD showing a characteristic peak at 278 nm. It was noticed that the CQD exhibit blue color under UV light illumination (365 nm). As per earlier reports, CQDs have absorption in the UV-region (260–320 nm) which is attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC bonds and the n-π* transition of CO bonds which coincides with the literature [18]. Fig. 1(b) shows the fluorescence emission behavior of the CQDs against different excitation wavelengths. It suggests that the CQD exhibit excitation dependent emission spectrum and the maximum emission intensity was notable at 440 nm given excitation of 360 nm wavelength [20]. Such behavior of the particles still remains inconclusive but it is assumed to be for reasons like various particle sizes, surface traps and surface defects. The Fig. 1(c) shows the TEM image of the CQD, the particles synthesized were found to be in different size ranges such as 1–4 nm and 30–40 nm. The size 30–40 nm diameter appears due to aggregation among the particles having colloidal nature (not shown here). The Fig. 1(d) shows the FTIR spectra of the as synthesized CQDs giving peaks at wavenumbers 650, 1548, 1696, 2369, 2936, 3146 which represent the presence of = C-H bond, NH bond, CO bond, CN group, CH stretch and O-H/N-H stretch respectively [[45,46]. The Fig. 1(e) represents the XRD pattern of the CQD. A broad peak is seen around 2(θ) of 250 referring to the plane (002) which suggests the amorphous nature of the particles due to the close packing of carbon atoms around alkyl chains [20]. Fig. 1(f) shows the Selected Area Energy Diffraction (SAED) pattern, which further confirms the amorphous nature of the CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HEWL amyloid inhibition and disaggregation study
ce:section.ce:para: The HEWL amyloid fibrils inhibition and disaggregation was performed with the as CQDs using ThT fluorescence assay. The HEWL protein when subjected to amyloid formation conditions in the presence and absence of CQD. The pH of the buffer was maintained acidic due to that pH 2.7 readily promotes the partial unfolding of HEWL molecules, this unfolding tends to have an important role in the formation of fibrils [47]. The as synthesized CQD were loaded in Sephadex G-25 column and the chromatogram is shown in Fig. 2(a). The Fig. 2(b) reveal ThT fluorescence intensity reduction in the samples incubated with HEWL and fractions of CQD. A volume of 300 μl from each fraction was added towards the amyloid inhibition. From the figure it is evident that fraction 6 showed maximum reduction in ThT fluorescence and hence was selected for further studies. The amyloid inhibition study with different concentrations of CQD was performed and the ThT fluorescence data is shown in Fig. 3(a). As evident from the figure, CQD exhibited concentration dependent inhibitory effects on HEWL amyloidogenesis with the optimum inhibition being nearby 50% for 350 μl of CQD. When compared the inhibitory effect of CQD with the reported inhibitors like 3-aminophenol, 4-aminophenol [48] and Graphene Quantum Dots [49], the results we obtained were in agreement with the reported literature. Apart from the amyloid inhibitory studies, effects of the CQDs on amyloid disaggregation was also studies as shown in Fig. 3(b). Here aliquots of the protein sample incubated in amyloidogenic conditions were taken at various time points and CQD were added and ThT fluorescence was measured after 1 h incubation time. As evident from the figure, CQDs showed significant disaggregation effects towards the aliquots collected at various stages of the aggregation process with the effect being more pronounced at the mature fibrillar stages showing almost 58% disaggregation. The HEWL amyloid fibril formation was also studied with the addition of CQD at incubation times such as 1 h, 2 h, 3 h separately. Further, we added CQDs at various stages of fibrillar growth and monitored its effect via ThT assay as shown in Fig. 4 . It was seen that as we delay the addition of CQD to the incubated protein its inhibitory effects also reduce significantly with CQD added after 3 h showing around 10% reduction in ThT fluorescence compared to those added at 1 h which showed around 30% reduction in ThT fluorescence. This shows that as the aggregation proceeds, the aggregated species become less susceptible to surrounding CQD. To further visualize the effects of the CQD on HEWL amyloidogenesis, FESEM imaging was done as shown in Fig. 5 . Early stage of aggregation also known as the pre-fibrillar stage shows more spherical type of aggregates as shown in Fig. 5 (a) whereas latter stage of aggregation known as mature fibrillar stage shows thinner and longer network of fibrils as seen in Fig. 5(c). In presence of CQD relatively lesser aggregated species were seen in both the pre-fibrillar as well as mature fibrillar stages shown in Figs. 5 (b) and 5 (d) respectively. This further confirms the inhibitory effects of the CQD towards HEWL amyloidogenesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HEWL amyloid fibril morphology and conformation
ce:section.ce:para: Circular dichroism (CD) was performed to monitor the structural changes in the protein on interaction with CQD. Data analyzed using the DichroWeb online server [50]. Fig. 6(a) shows the Far UV CD spectra of monomeric and fibrillar HEWL in presence and absence of CQD. The percentage secondary structural predictions of HEWL in presence and absence of CQD is shown in Table 1 . As amyloidogenesis proceeds there is a prominent reduction in the α-helical conformation at 209 nm and a new peak was observed around 217 nm which is the characteristic peak for the β-sheet [51]. However, in presence of CQD a lesser intensity peak can be seen around 217 nm possible reason could be due to the reduction in β-sheet conformation in the fibrils and more positive peak around 210 nm could be due to the increase in the content of unordered structures. The CD data is further correlated with the FTIR data shown in Fig. 6(b). From the figure, the amide band I (usually appears in the range of 1600–1700 cm−1, the CO stretching of the peptide bond) at 1650 cm−1 indicate the presence of beta-sheet conformation in the HEWL fibril. In presence of CQD, the amide band I appear at 1657 cm−1 and increase in peak intensity for amide band II (appear between 1500 and 1600 cm−1, the NH bending mode of vibration in peptide bond) at 1522 cm−1 could indicate increase in the content of α-helix or random coil conformation in the complex [52,53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: HEWL and CQD interaction study using fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para: To further get insight on the interaction between the CQD and HEWL fluorescence quenching studies were performed, as shown in Fig. 7 . When a sample containing both HEWL and the CQD were selectively excited for tryptophan (trp) at 295 nm, it was observed the trp emission gradually decreased with increase in time duration after excitation and the CQD emission which showed λmax at around 440 nm increased with increase in time duration after excitation. This clearly indicates occurrence of Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the HEWL-CQD complex. Thus, it can be undeniably concluded that the CQD are forming a strong interaction with HEWL, where they are close enough to quench HEWL trp emission through FRET. The Fig. 7(b) show the Stern-Volmer plot for HEWL and CQD. The graph showing positive deviation from linearity suggests that quenching was not purely dynamic but could be both dynamic and static quenching. The main mechanism of quenching could be static quenching, where formation of a stable complex of HEWL-CQD occurs at ground state. This is evident from the decrease in quenching rate with increase in temperature as obtained from the Stern-Volmer plot [51,54]. Table 2 shows the predicted quenching rate constant (Kq) and number of binding sites (n) between HEWL and CQD at different temperatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Equilibrium unfolding of HEWL protein in presence of CQD
ce:section.ce:para: In an attempt to elucidate the thermodynamic basis of amyloid inhibition, guanidine hydrochloride (GuHCl) mediated equilibrium unfolding studies were performed on HEWL in presence and absence of the CQDs, as shown in from Fig. 8 . The CQD on interaction with HEWL seem to stabilize the protein as evident from the red shift in the equilibrium unfolding graph (the transition midpoint of unfolding, Cm shifted from 3 M approx. to 3.5 M approx. on addition of the CQDs). In order to relate the stabilizing effect of the CQD with their anti-amyloidogenic effect on HEWL, an energy diagram is provided in Fig. 8(b). For an amyloidogenic protein to form amyloid, it must overcome certain energy barrier/activation energy represented by Ea. The CQDs act by stabilizing HEWL, thereby increasing the required Ea and hence suppressing the aggregation process [55].

In the current study, CQD in the range of 1–40 nm were synthesized from readily available kitchen spice mix. The as-synthesized CQDs were characterized using UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR spectroscopy, X ray diffraction and TEM which successful synthesis of the CQD which were found to be amorphous in nature. The CQD when subjected to anti-amyloidogenic studies against HEWL, exhibited amyloid inhibitory as well as disaggregation effects on the protein with almost 50% amyloid inhibition. The amyloid inhibition effect was further confirmed by TEM which exhibited relative lesser aggregated or fibrillar species in the pre-fibrillar and the mature fibrillar stages respectively, in the presence of the CQD. The inhibitory effects were found to be more prominent when the CQD were added at the start of the aggregation process and it got reduced on delaying the addition of the CQD. Both circular dichroism as well as FTIR studies reveal on incubation with CQD the structural changes in HEWL were driven more towards formation of random coils whereas, in the control sample there was more beta sheet formation. Fluorescence quenching studies reveal occurrence of FRET between HEWL and the CQD suggesting formation of a close complex between them. Stern-Volmer plot also reveals occurrence of both static as well as dynamic quenching. The decrease in the Stern-Volmer plot on increasing temperature indicates predominant static quenching. Stability studies using guanidine hydrochloride mediated equilibrium unfolding clearly shows the stabilizing effect of the CQD on HEWL. It can be concluded that the CQD on interacting with HEWL is forming a stable complex thereby increasing the activation energy of the amyloidogenesis and hence resisting the process of aggregation. A schematic representation demonstrating the inhibitory effects of CQD on HEWL amyloidogenesis is shown in Fig. 9 . Heterogeneity in the sizes of the CQDs due to aggregation could be possible limitation when exploring further for In-vivo studies. This can be overcome by surface modification of the CQDs to increase their stability. Further the cytotoxicity associated with the use of CQDs as therapeutic agents need to be further studied. Thus, the overall studies demonstrate the potential of CQD in inhibiting as well as disaggregating amyloid fibrils and hence they can be explored further as effective therapeutics against human lysozyme amyloidosis and other types under in-vitro and in-vivo conditions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

Dr. Nandini Sarkar reports financial support was provided by National Institute of Technology Rourkela. Dr. Nandini Sarkar reports a relationship with National Institute of Technology Rourkela that includes: employment.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors acknowledgement the funding provided to NS by Science & Engineering Research Board (SERB), Govt. of India (Grant Number ECR/2017/002081). The infrastructural facilities provided by NIT Rourkela are also acknowledged.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: HX-ESI-MS and optical studies of the unfolding of thioredoxin indicate stabilization of a partially unfolded, aggregation-component intermediate at low pH

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Hamid Wani, J.B. Udgaonkar, HX-ESI-MS and optical studies of the unfolding of thioredoxin indicate stabilization of a partially unfolded, aggregation-component intermediate at low pH, Biochemistry. 45 (2006) 11226-11238. doi:10.1021/bi060647h.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amyloid oligomers: A joint experimental/computational perspective on Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, Type-II diabetes, and Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Phuong Nguyen, Ayyalusamy Ramamoorthy, R. Bikash Sahoo, Jie Zheng, Peter Faller, E. John Straub, Laura Dominguez, Joan-Emma Shea, V. Nikolay Dokholyan, Alfonso De Simone, Buyong Ma, Ruth Nussinov, Saeed Najafi, Son Tung Ngo, Antonie Loquet, Mara Chiricotto, Pritam Ganguly, James McCarty, Mai Suan Li, Carol Hall, Yiming Wang, Yifat Miller, Simone Melchionna, Birgit Habenstein, Stepan Time, Jiaxing Chen, Brianna Hnath, Birgit Strodel, Rakez Kayed, Sylvain Lesne, Guanghong Wei, Fabio Sterpone, J. Andrew Doig, Philippe Derreumaux, Amyloid oligomers: A joint experimental/computational perspective on Alzheimer'’s disease, Parkinson'’s disease, Type-II diabetes, and Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, Chem. Rev. 121 (2021) 2545-2647. doi:10.1021/acs.chem.rev.0c01122.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Identification of the core structure of lysozyme amyloid fibrils by proteolysis
